rule: the SMALLEST restart count that begins a PLATEAU -- one whose derived probe count is matched by every larger point on the grid. Deliberately NOT 'the restart count with the best derived nprobe', which is the obvious rule and a trap: this sweep is jagged (1-2 restarts need 7 probes, 3 needs only 3, and 5 through 25 all settle on 6), so the winner-takes-all rule would pick 3 -- a single lucky draw that no larger restart count reproduces. We are choosing a METHOD, not a lottery ticket, and selecting the luckiest point on the grid would reintroduce exactly the dependence on a fortunate init that this constant exists to remove. A plateau is the signature of a method that has stopped depending on its draw. Note also that the objective is NOT inertia: inertia is what each restart minimizes internally, and best-by-inertia is not best-by-recall, because a tighter fit in the k-means sense can be a more unbalanced one in the IVF sense -- and IVF is what a query pays for. Smallest on the plateau, because training cost is linear in this and the plateau is flat.
note: S5. This constant became TUNED the moment it was chosen by measurement, and it was chosen because fixing charter C-4 (the training sample keyed on event_id rather than on physical position) removed a LUCKY INPUT ORDER that k-means++ had been quietly relying on -- recall fell below its floor, and the fix was to stop depending on a draw. Corpus- AND generation-conditional: a different corpus or a different embedder will have a different answer.
